questions.question: Explain why wall-clock time rather than CPU time is the correct metric for timing parallel MIMD programs.
keyPoints: Wall-clock time captures the full elapsed time including communication, synchronization, and idle waiting
CPU time only measures active computation and misses parallel overhead
In MIMD programs, processes spend significant time waiting for messages and synchronization
MPI_Wtime provides portable, high-resolution wall-clock timing

questions.question: Describe the two-barrier approach for timing MPI programs and explain the importance of each barrier.
keyPoints: First barrier synchronizes all processes before timing starts
Without first barrier, fast processes begin timing early while slow ones still initialize
Second barrier ensures all processes complete work before timing ends
Without second barrier, master may stop timing before workers finish

questions.question: Write the MPI timing code pattern using MPI_Wtime and barriers, and explain each step. Also discuss how to aggregate timing data from multiple processes.
keyPoints: Call MPI_Barrier to synchronize all processes before timing
Call start = MPI_Wtime() to record start time
Execute parallel computation and communication
Call MPI_Barrier to ensure all processes complete
Call end = MPI_Wtime() to record end time
elapsed = end - start gives wall-clock time on rank 0
Use MPI_Reduce with MPI_MAX to find the maximum time across all processes
Run multiple trials for statistical reliability

questions.question: Discuss best practices for obtaining reliable and meaningful timing measurements of MIMD parallel programs.
keyPoints: Run multiple trials and report average or minimum time
Separate initialization time from parallel computation time
Use MPI_Wtime for portable high-resolution timing
Account for timer overhead on very short runs
Use MPI_Reduce to aggregate timing across processes
Time only the parallel section of interest
